topic: What system lets many concurrent AI coding sessions commit and push to one shared long-lived branch (e.g. v4) continuously, with zero operator-visible divergence blockers (no 'N commits behind', no non-fast-forward rejections), an always-current local checkout in the IDE, and no loss of any session's concurrent uncommitted work?

focus: Git publication races and worktree-independent ref construction
noveltyJustification: First evidence pass established the primitive-level correctness boundary and ruled out blind force push.
keyQuestions: Which integration strategy handles concurrent publication and push races with the strongest correctness and liveness properties?
Which workspace model prevents sessions from overwriting each other's files while keeping the shared branch current?
How can a local IDE checkout stay current without disturbing uncommitted changes?
Which invariants prevent orphaned autostashes, overwritten work, force-push loss, and make rollback reliable?

ruledOut.reason: Git documents that it can lose remote commits

ruledOut.approach: scratch index as an automatic conflict solver
ruledOut.reason: read-tree can leave conflicting paths unresolved

graphEvents.label: Serialized publisher with explicit ref CAS
Scratch index keeps IDE worktree untouched
Linked worktrees isolate index and files

focus: Safe checkout freshness, autostash failure modes, guarded updates, and rollback
noveltyJustification: Established the fetch-only versus working-tree freshness boundary and documented why autostash and destructive reset cannot provide a no-loss invariant.
keyQuestions: How can a local IDE checkout stay current without disturbing uncommitted changes?
Which invariants prevent orphaned autostashes, overwritten work, force-push loss, and make rollback reliable?
Which automation surface provides the right lifecycle, observability, and failure recovery characteristics?
answeredQuestions: How can a local IDE checkout stay current without disturbing uncommitted changes?

ruledOut.approach: blind pull --rebase --autostash in a dirty shared IDE worktree
ruledOut.reason: Autostash application can conflict and temporary stash state can remain after quit

ruledOut.approach: blind git reset --hard for checkout freshness
ruledOut.reason: Can overwrite tracked edits and remove obstructing untracked files

ruledOut.approach: fast-forward-only as the complete integration strategy
ruledOut.reason: Refuses when local and incoming histories diverge

graphEvents.label: Fetch-only watcher refreshes refs without rewriting the worktree
Fast-forward-only update belongs on a clean current-view worktree
Autostash needs identity tracking and post-apply verification
reset --keep refuses overlap while reset --hard can lose work
Always-current files and lossless arbitrary dirty edits require separate ownership
Rollback needs explicit old state and retained recovery material

focus: Automation surfaces and existing art for serialized publication and recovery
noveltyJustification: Compared local hooks, wrappers, daemons, remote queues, and adjacent tools; identified the daemon plus clean publisher as the low-latency surface and merge queues/Gerrit as stronger CI-gated remote analogues.
keyQuestions: Which automation surface provides the right lifecycle, observability, and failure recovery characteristics?
What do the named existing tools and workflows demonstrate or fail to guarantee?
Which conflict-avoidance and conflict-handling rules make concurrent additive work tractable?
answeredQuestions: Which automation surface provides the right lifecycle, observability, and failure recovery characteristics?

ruledOut.approach: client hooks as the sole serializer
ruledOut.reason: Hooks can reject or notify but do not provide durable shared queue ownership
The cited action explicitly uses force push and requires a mirror backup

ruledOut.approach: Syncthing-style file mirroring as Git integration
ruledOut.reason: Replicates files but does not establish Git ref parentage or fast-forward publication

graphEvents.label: Hooks are guardrails, not the multi-writer coordinator
Launch wrapper allocates isolated workspaces but cannot serialize publication
Background daemon is the right local queue and recovery surface
GitHub merge queue serializes tested PR integration
Gerrit provides pending changes and submit requirements
Git Town demonstrates stash and undo UX without a shared multiplexer
mob, git-sync, git-auto-sync, and Syncthing solve adjacent bounded problems

focus: Conflict avoidance and handling policies with non-destructive merge preflight
noveltyJustification: Converted Git merge-tree, rerere, merge-driver, and sparse-checkout semantics into explicit conflict rules and an executable acceptance suite.
keyQuestions: Which conflict-avoidance and conflict-handling rules make concurrent additive work tractable?
Which integration strategy should be the default when low latency matters more than CI-gated queueing?
Which safety and rollback invariants must be made acceptance tests rather than documentation claims?
answeredQuestions: Which conflict-avoidance and conflict-handling rules make concurrent additive work tractable?

ruledOut.approach: additive-only without unique paths or keys
ruledOut.reason: Shared generated files, duplicate names, renames, and deletes can still conflict
Projects files but does not isolate refs or ownership

ruledOut.approach: unconditional rerere or ours/theirs merge drivers
ruledOut.reason: Can encode an unverified semantic choice

graphEvents.label: Disjoint path ownership is the strongest structural conflict avoidance rule
Per-session subtrees help only with unique paths or keys
merge-tree provides non-destructive clean/conflicted merge preflight
rerere accelerates repeated resolutions but is not correctness proof
Custom merge drivers need fail-closed domain policy
Sparse checkout is a projection optimization, not ref isolation
Acceptance tests must verify no silent loss, durable conflicts, CAS, and rollback

focus: Adversarial architecture review, crash recovery, and final acceptance matrix
noveltyJustification: Stress-tested the candidate architectures against crash windows, ambiguous push acknowledgments, object reachability, rollback, and the exact boundary of the no-loss contract.
keyQuestions: Which integration strategy handles concurrent publication and push races with the strongest correctness and liveness properties?
Which workspace model prevents sessions from overwriting each other's files while keeping the shared branch current?
How can a local IDE checkout stay current without disturbing uncommitted changes?
Which invariants prevent orphaned autostashes, overwritten work, force-push loss, and make rollback reliable?
Which automation surface provides the right lifecycle, observability, and failure recovery characteristics?
Which conflict-avoidance and conflict-handling rules make concurrent additive work tractable?
What do the named existing tools and workflows demonstrate or fail to guarantee?
answeredQuestions: Which integration strategy handles concurrent publication and push races with the strongest correctness and liveness properties?
Which workspace model prevents sessions from overwriting each other's files while keeping the shared branch current?
How can a local IDE checkout stay current without disturbing uncommitted changes?
Which invariants prevent orphaned autostashes, overwritten work, force-push loss, and make rollback reliable?
Which automation surface provides the right lifecycle, observability, and failure recovery characteristics?
Which conflict-avoidance and conflict-handling rules make concurrent additive work tractable?
What do the named existing tools and workflows demonstrate or fail to guarantee?

ruledOut.approach: independent session push loops as the default
ruledOut.reason: Cannot own durable retries or hide races without central coordination

ruledOut.approach: git fsck discovery as the durable queue
ruledOut.reason: Recovery scanning is not submission ownership

ruledOut.approach: including unsaved editor buffers in the Git no-loss guarantee
ruledOut.reason: Git cannot observe state never written to disk

graphEvents.label: Isolated session worktrees plus one serialized merge-tree/CAS publisher is the low-latency default
Independent retry, serialized daemon, remote queue, and scratch publisher have distinct trade-offs
Pending refs or bundles are needed across crashes before final publication
Ambiguous push acknowledgments require read-after-write reconciliation
No-loss covers saved managed files, not unsaved buffers or arbitrary semantic overlap
Acceptance spans races, liveness, overlap, dirty worktrees, crash, rollback, and observability
